narrative_blocks.header: His Delaware wife outlived him by twenty-eight years
narrative_blocks.body: Ray married Gertrude Newbold Comly of Edgemoor, Delaware in 1925 — she was the daughter of a Quaker Delaware family. Gertrude outlived Ray by twenty-eight years, dying in Medford, New Jersey in 1990 at the remarkable age of one hundred and one.

sources.short: Werner v. Werner, 195 Ga. 868 (1943) — named defendant

narrative_blocks.header: His Delaware wife outlived him by twenty-eight years
narrative_blocks.body: Ray married Gertrude Newbold Comly of Edgemoor, Delaware in 1925 — she was the daughter of a Quaker Delaware family. Gertrude outlived Ray by twenty-eight years, dying in Medford, New Jersey in 1990 at the remarkable age of one hundred and one.

sources.short: Werner v. Werner, 195 Ga. 868 (1943) — named defendant

burial.plot_notes: Oakland Cemetery, Atlanta — buried with husband Ray Cowles Werner despite dying in New Jersey (pre-arranged or family decision)

narrative_blocks.header: Quaker-heritage Delaware woman who outlived her husband
narrative_blocks.body: Born in Edgemoor, Delaware to a Quaker-heritage Comly family, Gertrude married Atlanta-born Ray Cowles Werner in 1925. She survived her husband by twenty-eight years, dying in Medford, New Jersey on May 1, 1990 at the age of one hundred and one — extraordinary_longevity. She was buried beside Ray at Oakland Cemetery in Atlanta.

career.title: Pioneer Atlanta citizen + railroad agent
career.organization: Macon & Western Railroad; later Georgia Railroad
career.notes: Arrived Atlanta 1845 at age 10. Originally with Macon & Western Railroad as chief clerk; remained through the Civil War. Later served as agent for the Georgia Railroad for many years. Said to be the first man to record a legal paper on the books of the City of Atlanta. 'Saw Atlanta build up from a little village.' At his 1896 death was one of Atlanta's oldest citizens.

landmarks.notes: Built Werner home on Washington Street, Atlanta 1870s; home demolished 1912.

narrative_blocks.header: Pioneer Atlanta citizen since 1845, Georgia Railroad agent
narrative_blocks.body: Edward arrived in Atlanta in 1845 when it was barely a village, began as Macon & Western Railroad chief clerk, and remained through the Civil War. He was said to be the first man to record a legal paper in the city's books. He served as Georgia Railroad agent for many years and was one of Atlanta's oldest citizens at his 1896 death.

sources.short: Find a Grave (E.A. Werner Sr, Oakland Cemetery Atlanta)
Atlanta Historical Society — E.A. Werner Home, Washington Street

burial.plot_notes: Oakland Cemetery, Atlanta — with husband Edward and all four children

education.notes: Per EH: 'educated at the Huntsville Female Seminary, Huntsville, Ala., a Presbyterian institution, which at that time was foremost in educating young ladies.'

institutions.notes: Per EH 1909, attended Huntsville Female Seminary.

narrative_blocks.header: Born in Georgia's capital, educated in Alabama
narrative_blocks.body: Florence was born March 6, 1843 in Milledgeville — Georgia's state capital — and educated at the Huntsville Female Seminary, a Presbyterian institution then foremost in educating young Southern women. She married Georgia Railroad agent Edward Werner in Huntsville in December 1872 and settled permanently in Atlanta.

narrative_blocks.header: Her estate sparked a Georgia Supreme Court battle
narrative_blocks.body: Florence died November 29, 1911, never having distributed her late husband Edward's estate to their four children. Her son Lynn assumed management without court authority; by 1943 the estate dispute reached the Georgia Supreme Court, where Edward Jr. sued Lynn, Florence, and Ray over property mismanagement and a disputed sale of 528 West Peachtree Street Atlanta.

sources.short: Werner v. Werner, 195 Ga. 868 (Ga. Sup. Ct. May 6, 1943, Reid CJ)
Edward Hooker 1909 — entry #1140a Ebenezer Mix Cowles

career.title: Merchant
career.notes: Merchant at Milledgeville Georgia per EH #1140a.

narrative_blocks.header: Lost his Southern fortune in the Civil War
narrative_blocks.body: Ebenezer had emigrated from Farmington, Connecticut to Milledgeville, Georgia before 1843 — Georgia's state capital and center of antebellum planter society. By 1860 the family had moved to Huntsville, Alabama. The Civil War erased everything he had built in the South, leaving his widow to support herself and their children alone.

narrative_blocks.header: His daughter and son-in-law anchored Atlanta's rail economy
narrative_blocks.body: His eldest daughter Florence married Edward A. Werner, a pioneer Atlanta citizen and Georgia Railroad agent who had arrived in Atlanta in 1845. His son Clifford Atwood got his first railroad job as a messenger in Werner's office in 1874, climbing through railroad management positions across the South for the next two decades.

narrative_blocks.header: Connecticut native who chose the cotton South
narrative_blocks.body: Ebenezer's 1840s move from Farmington, Connecticut to Milledgeville — Georgia's antebellum state capital — followed a pattern of Northern professionals drawn to the cotton-and-rail economy emerging in the Deep South. The choice ran counter to other branches of the Cowles family: his abolitionist Cleveland kinsmen worked the Underground Railroad while he built a life in slaveholding Georgia. The Civil War ended his Southern enterprise and the inheritance it might have left.

narrative_blocks.header: Her Atwood family gave her children Mayflower descent
narrative_blocks.body: Frances's maiden name Atwood gave her son Clifford his middle name and the family its Mayflower Descendants membership — he was ninth in descent from Pilgrim Richard Warren. She is the ancestral source of the Cowles-Atwood Mayflower thread that runs through Florence, Irene, and Clifford's lines.

narrative_blocks.header: Raised three children alone after the Civil War
narrative_blocks.body: When the Civil War stripped Ebenezer of everything he had built in Georgia and Alabama, Frances was left to support herself and their children. Her eldest daughter Florence's marriage to Georgia Railroad agent Edward Werner in 1872 became the family's economic anchor in the post-war years.

sources.short: Edward Hooker 1909 — entry #1140a Ebenezer Mix Cowles

burialCemetery.name: Oakland Cemetery
burialCemetery.notes: Historic Atlanta cemetery founded 1850. Burial place of the Werner-Cowles family branch: Florence Woodruff Cowles Werner (HD3774), her husband Edward A. Werner Sr (I00425), and all four of their children — Edward Jr (HD3775), Lynn Woodruff (HD3776), Florence Brine (HD3777), Ray Cowles (HD3778) — plus Ray's wife Gertrude Comly Werner (I00427). Contains notable Atlanta citizens including 6 governors, Margaret Mitchell, Bobby Jones.
